draft.introduction: Chapter 16 distinguishes divine from demonic natures. The divine nature leads to liberation; the demonic—characterized by hypocrisy, arrogance, and lust—leads to bondage.

translation.text: The Blessed Lord said: Fearlessness, purity of heart, steadfastness in knowledge and yoga, charity, self-control, sacrifice, study of the scriptures, austerity, straightforwardness—these belong to one born with divine nature. Hypocrisy, arrogance, pride, anger, harshness, and ignorance belong to one born with demonic nature.
translation.original_text: śrī bhagavān uvāca | abhayaṃ sattva-saṃśuddhir jñāna-yoga-vyavasthitiḥ

translator_notes.note: The daivī/āsurī distinction is not about cosmological beings but about psychological orientations. Everyone contains both tendencies. The chapter’s practical force is diagnostic: it invites the reader to recognize which qualities dominate their own character.
